avoid: Replacing cuBLAS for W @ X with a custom GEMM kernel.
Hardcoding hidden dimensions or assuming fixed tensor sizes.
Introducing additional intermediate tensors that increase memory traffic.
evidence: Correctness is perfect (max_abs_err=0.0, rel_l2_err=0.0) for all shapes.
Aggregate speedup is 1.01x, essentially identical to the PyTorch baseline.
Speedup per shape: 3584: 1.04x, 4096: 1.00x, 4608: 0.998x, indicating no meaningful improvement.
Profile summary identifies bottleneck as multiple ATen GEMM and add launches, which is exactly the baseline implementation.
The rank-16 update (A @ (B.T @ X)) involves two small GEMMs and an addition, causing launch overhead and memory bandwidth inefficiency.
The large W @ X GEMM is already handled by cuBLAS and is not the bottleneck.

recommended_actions: fuse rank-16 update into the output tensor
test scalar and vectorized rank-16 kernels
Fuse the rank-16 update into a single kernel: compute B.T @ X and then A @ result in one pass, writing directly to the output to avoid intermediate allocations and reduce kernel launches.
Keep cuBLAS for the large W @ X GEMM; do not attempt a custom full GEMM.
Consider vectorized memory accesses and unrolling for the fused rank-16 kernel to improve memory bandwidth utilization.
If shape-dependent performance varies significantly in future candidates, implement shape-aware dispatch to select between fused and baseline paths.
